name: Staphylococcus aureus
morphology: Catalase +, coagulase +, beta-hemolytic, golden-yellow colonies, ferments mannitol (yellow on MSA), protein A
diseases: Skin/soft tissue infx, abscesses, acute endocarditis (tricuspid in IVDU), osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, pneumonia, food poisoning, TSS, scalded skin syndrome
treatment: MSSA: nafcillin/oxacillin/cefazolin. MRSA: vancomycin, linezolid, or daptomycin
pearl: Coagulase + clusters; preformed enterotoxin (heat-stable) → rapid food poisoning; TSST-1 superantigen; protein A binds Fc of IgG to evade opsonization/phagocytosis

name: Staphylococcus epidermidis
morphology: Catalase +, coagulase NEGATIVE, novobiocin SENSITIVE, forms biofilm (extracellular polysaccharide slime), urease +; white colonies, non-hemolytic
diseases: Prosthetic device/joint & IV catheter infections, prosthetic valve endocarditis; frequent blood culture contaminant (normal skin flora)
treatment: Vancomycin (usually methicillin-resistant); remove infected hardware/catheter
pearl: Biofilm on indwelling devices/catheters; coag-negative + novobiocin SENSITIVE = epidermidis

name: Staphylococcus saprophyticus
morphology: Catalase +, coagulase NEGATIVE, novobiocin RESISTANT, urease +
diseases: 2nd most common cause of uncomplicated UTI/cystitis in sexually active young women (E. coli is #1)
treatment: TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin (uncomplicated cystitis)
pearl: Coag-negative + novobiocin RESISTANT = saprophyticus; 'honeymoon cystitis' in young women

name: Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A strep)
morphology: Catalase NEGATIVE, beta-hemolytic, bacitracin SENSITIVE, PYR +, Lancefield group A
diseases: Pharyngitis, scarlet fever, impetigo, erysipelas, cellulitis, necrotizing fasciitis, strep TSS; sequelae: rheumatic fever, PSGN
treatment: Penicillin or amoxicillin (no documented penicillin resistance); add clindamycin for severe/necrotizing/TSS (toxin suppression)
pearl: Bacitracin sensitive, PYR +; M protein = antiphagocytic + molecular mimicry → rheumatic fever; ASO/anti-DNase B titers; PSGN not prevented by treatment

name: Streptococcus agalactiae (Group B strep)
morphology: Catalase NEGATIVE, beta-hemolytic, bacitracin RESISTANT, CAMP test +, hippurate hydrolysis +, Lancefield group B
diseases: Neonatal meningitis, pneumonia & sepsis; chorioamnionitis/postpartum infx; UTI/sepsis/soft tissue infx in elderly & diabetics
treatment: Penicillin/ampicillin; intrapartum penicillin prophylaxis for colonized mothers
pearl: CAMP test + (arrowhead-shaped enhancement near S. aureus), hippurate +; rectovaginal screen of pregnant women at 36-37 wks (updated from 35-37)

name: Streptococcus pneumoniae
morphology: Catalase NEGATIVE, alpha-hemolytic, optochin SENSITIVE, bile SOLUBLE, polysaccharide capsule (quellung +), IgA protease
diseases: #1 cause of community-acquired pneumonia, otitis media & sinusitis (kids), adult bacterial meningitis; sepsis (esp. asplenic/sickle cell)
treatment: Ceftriaxone (+ vancomycin for meningitis); amoxicillin for non-severe; vaccines PCV (e.g., PCV15/20) and PPSV23
pearl: MOPS (Meningitis, Otitis media, Pneumonia, Sinusitis); optochin sensitive + bile soluble; rusty sputum; encapsulated → overwhelming sepsis in asplenia

name: Streptococcus viridans group (e.g., S. mutans, S. sanguinis, S. mitis)
morphology: Catalase NEGATIVE, alpha-hemolytic, optochin RESISTANT, bile INSOLUBLE
diseases: Subacute bacterial endocarditis (on damaged/prosthetic valves), dental caries (S. mutans), brain/dental abscess
treatment: Penicillin G (+ gentamicin) for endocarditis
pearl: Optochin RESISTANT + bile INSOLUBLE (distinguishes from pneumococcus); S. sanguinis makes dextrans → adheres to fibrin-platelet vegetations after dental work; S. mutans → caries

name: Enterococcus faecalis / faecium
morphology: Catalase NEGATIVE, gamma (non)-hemolytic, PYR +, Lancefield group D, grows in 6.5% NaCl & bile-esculin +
diseases: UTI, biliary tract infections, subacute endocarditis (esp. after GI/GU procedures), nosocomial bacteremia/line infx
treatment: Ampicillin + aminoglycoside (gentamicin) synergy; VRE: linezolid or daptomycin
pearl: Grows in bile + 6.5% NaCl (hardy gut flora); VRE = vanA (D-ala-D-lac); E. faecium more resistant than E. faecalis (incl. VRE)

name: Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis biotype I)
morphology: Catalase NEGATIVE, gamma-hemolytic, Lancefield group D, bile-esculin +, grows in bile but NOT in 6.5% NaCl
diseases: Bacteremia & endocarditis associated with colorectal cancer; also colonic adenoma/IBD
treatment: Penicillin G (+/- aminoglycoside); colonoscopy to evaluate for colorectal malignancy
pearl: 'Bovis in the blood = cancer in the colon'; group D but salt-intolerant (6.5% NaCl negative, unlike Enterococcus)

name: Staphylococcus lugdunensis
morphology: Catalase +, tube coagulase NEGATIVE (slide/clumping factor may be +); behaves aggressively like S. aureus
diseases: Aggressive native & prosthetic valve endocarditis (often destructive), skin/soft tissue & prosthetic device infections
treatment: Often beta-lactam susceptible (penicillin/nafcillin) but treat aggressively; valve surgery often required
pearl: Coag-negative staph that behaves like S. aureus — virulent, destructive endocarditis; PYR + and ornithine decarboxylase +

name: Streptococcus anginosus group (S. anginosus, intermedius, constellatus)
morphology: Catalase NEGATIVE, variable hemolysis (alpha/beta/gamma), small colonies, caramel/butterscotch (honey) odor
diseases: Pyogenic abscesses (brain, liver, dental, abdominal), empyema; bacteremia
treatment: Penicillin/ceftriaxone +/- metronidazole; drain abscess (source control)
pearl: Abscess-forming 'milleri/anginosus' group; butterscotch/caramel smell; think pus collections

name: Clostridium tetani
morphology: Terminal spores -> 'tennis racket'/drumstick appearance; motile (peritrichous); grows on anaerobic blood agar
diseases: Tetanus (spastic paralysis): lockjaw/trismus, risus sardonicus, opisthotonos; from puncture wounds/soil
treatment: Antitoxin (human TIG), metronidazole or penicillin, wound debridement, benzodiazepines; toxoid vaccine (DTaP/Tdap)
pearl: Tetanospasmin (a zinc protease) cleaves SNARE (synaptobrevin/VAMP), blocking GABA/glycine release from Renshaw-cell inhibitory neurons -> spastic paralysis.

name: Clostridium botulinum
morphology: Subterminal spores; anaerobic; toxin is heat-LABILE (destroyed by boiling ~10 min); spores are heat-resistant
diseases: Botulism: symmetric DESCENDING flaccid paralysis, diplopia, dysphagia, dry mouth. Floppy baby (honey). Canned/home-preserved foods
treatment: Antitoxin (equine heptavalent) for adults; infant: human-derived BabyBIG; supportive mechanical ventilation
pearl: Toxin (zinc protease) cleaves SNARE proteins -> blocks ACh release at NMJ. '4 D's': diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria, dysphonia. Honey -> infants (<1 yr).

name: Clostridium perfringens
morphology: Double zone of hemolysis on blood agar; 'stormy fermentation' in litmus milk; boxcar-shaped rods; spores rarely seen
diseases: Gas gangrene (myonecrosis, crepitus), late-onset food poisoning (reheated meat), clostridial cellulitis, enteritis necroticans
treatment: Surgical debridement, penicillin + clindamycin; hyperbaric O2 adjunct
pearl: Alpha toxin = lecithinase (phospholipase C) -> myonecrosis & crepitant gas. Food poisoning self-limited ~24h; one of the most common causes of US foodborne illness.

name: Clostridioides difficile
morphology: Spores resistant to alcohol gel (use soap/water); diagnose with stool GDH + toxin EIA and/or NAAT/PCR (toxins A & B)
diseases: Pseudomembranous colitis, antibiotic-associated diarrhea; toxic megacolon
treatment: Oral fidaxomicin or oral vancomycin (both 1st-line; fidaxomicin preferred); stop offending antibiotic; FMT for recurrence
pearl: Follows clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins, PPIs. Toxin A=enterotoxin, B=cytotoxin (B is the key virulence factor). Pseudomembranes on colonoscopy.

name: Bacillus anthracis
type: Gram-positive rod, spore-forming, aerobic (facultative anaerobe); NON-motile
morphology: Boxcar rods in long chains ('serpentine/medusa-head' colonies); poly-D-glutamate (polypeptide) capsule; non-hemolytic on blood agar
diseases: Cutaneous anthrax (painless black eschar), inhalational 'woolsorter's disease' (mediastinal widening, hemorrhagic mediastinitis), GI anthrax
treatment: Ciprofloxacin or doxycycline; add antitoxin (raxibacumab) for systemic disease; bioterror agent
pearl: Only bacterium with a protein (poly-D-glutamate) capsule. Tripartite toxin: protective antigen (PA) + edema factor (calmodulin-dependent adenylate cyclase) + lethal factor (zinc protease/MAPKK protease).

name: Bacillus cereus
type: Gram-positive rod, spore-forming, aerobic (facultative anaerobe); motile
morphology: Beta-hemolytic, motile; spores survive cooking/reheating
diseases: Food poisoning: emetic type (reheated fried rice) & diarrheal type (meats/sauces); rare keratitis/endophthalmitis (esp. IV drug use, trauma)
treatment: Supportive/self-limited; for severe/ocular: vancomycin or clindamycin (resistant to beta-lactams via beta-lactamase)
pearl: 'Reheated fried rice' -> heat-STABLE preformed cerulide toxin -> emesis in 1-5h. Diarrheal (heat-labile) enterotoxin acts like cholera/E. coli LT (cAMP), onset 8-18h.

name: Listeria monocytogenes
type: Gram-positive rod, facultative intracellular, non-spore-forming, facultative anaerobe
morphology: Tumbling motility (22-28C, not 37C); cold growth (4C, 'cold enrichment'); narrow zone of beta-hemolysis; catalase +
diseases: Neonatal meningitis/sepsis (granulomatosis infantiseptica), meningitis in elderly/immunosuppressed/neonates, febrile gastroenteritis, amnionitis/abortion in pregnancy
treatment: Ampicillin (+/- gentamicin); intrinsically resistant to ALL cephalosporins
pearl: Uses ActA to polymerize host actin 'rocket tails' for cell-to-cell spread (avoids antibody). Unpasteurized dairy, deli meats, soft cheeses. Only GP rod producing endotoxin (LPS)-like LTA; pregnancy & immunocompromised risk.

name: Corynebacterium diphtheriae
type: Gram-positive rod (club-shaped), non-spore-forming, aerobic (facultative anaerobe); non-motile
morphology: Club-shaped, 'Chinese letter'/palisade arrangement; metachromatic granules (Babes-Ernst/volutin) on Loeffler medium; black/gray colonies on cystine-tellurite agar
diseases: Diphtheria: gray pseudomembrane on pharynx/tonsils, 'bull neck' lymphadenopathy, myocarditis, neuropathy (e.g., palatal/recurrent laryngeal palsy)
treatment: Diphtheria antitoxin FIRST, then penicillin or erythromycin; prevent with toxoid (DTaP/Tdap)
pearl: Exotoxin (beta-corynephage/lysogen encoded) ADP-ribosylates EF-2 -> halts protein synthesis (same target as Pseudomonas exotoxin A). Elek test detects toxin. 'ABCDEFG': ADP-ribosylation, Beta-prophage, Corynebacterium, Diphtheriae, Elongation Factor 2, Granules.

name: Actinomyces israelii
morphology: Branching filaments; yellow 'sulfur granules' (microcolonies) in pus/sinus tracts; slow-growing anaerobe; NOT acid-fast
diseases: Cervicofacial abscess ('lumpy jaw') after dental work/trauma, thoracic & abdominopelvic actinomycosis (IUDs), draining sinus tracts
treatment: Prolonged high-dose penicillin G (weeks-months); surgical drainage of abscesses
pearl: 'Sulfur granules' + normal oral/GI flora. Mnemonic: Actinomyces=Penicillin / Nocardia=Sulfonamides. Anaerobe, NOT acid-fast (vs aerobic, partially acid-fast Nocardia).

name: Nocardia (asteroides complex / N. brasiliensis)
type: Gram-positive branching rod (filamentous), strict aerobe, partially (weakly) acid-fast
morphology: Partially acid-fast (modified Kinyoun/Fite, acid-fast with weak decolorizer); thin beaded branching filaments; aerobic; catalase + & urease +
diseases: Pulmonary nocardiosis (cavitary, immunocompromised), hematogenous brain abscess, cutaneous/lymphocutaneous (soil; N. brasiliensis in gardeners)
treatment: TMP-SMX (sulfonamides) first-line; add imipenem/amikacin for severe/CNS disease
pearl: Partially acid-fast aerobe in immunocompromised/transplant/chronic steroid pts; classic brain abscess. Mnemonic: SNAP - Sulfonamides, Nocardia, Acid-fast (partial), Pulmonary/aerobe.

name: Clostridium septicum
type: Gram-positive rod, spore-forming, obligate anaerobe; motile
morphology: Anaerobic; produces gas; alpha-toxin (pore-forming); subterminal spores
diseases: Spontaneous (atraumatic, non-traumatic) gas gangrene/myonecrosis; bacteremia strongly linked to occult colon cancer
treatment: Penicillin + clindamycin, aggressive surgical debridement; evaluate for colorectal malignancy
pearl: C. septicum bacteremia/atraumatic myonecrosis = red flag -> work up for colorectal cancer or hematologic malignancy (neutropenia facilitates gut translocation).

name: Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium acnes)
morphology: Slow-growing diphtheroid; lipophilic; normal skin flora; catalase + & indole +
diseases: Acne vulgaris; prosthetic-joint (esp. shoulder), spinal hardware and CSF-shunt infections; common blood-culture contaminant
treatment: Acne: topical/oral antibiotics (e.g., clindamycin/doxycycline) + retinoids; deep infection: penicillin or vancomycin + hardware removal
pearl: Anaerobic diphtheroid colonizing sebaceous glands; drives acne via inflammation & comedogenesis. Indolent prosthetic shoulder-joint infections (long incubation cultures). Frequent contaminant.

name: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae
type: Gram-positive rod, non-spore-forming, facultative anaerobe; non-motile
morphology: Thin pleomorphic rods, often in chains; H2S production on TSI (rare for a GPR); catalase NEGATIVE (vs Listeria catalase +)
diseases: Erysipeloid (violaceous, well-demarcated hand/finger cellulitis) in fishermen/butchers/vets; rarely septicemia and endocarditis
treatment: Penicillin (1st-line); intrinsically resistant to vancomycin
pearl: 'Fish/swine handler' violaceous finger lesion. Catalase-NEGATIVE, H2S+, non-motile GPR; intrinsically vancomycin-resistant (notable, since most GP organisms are vanc-susceptible).

name: Neisseria meningitidis
morphology: Ferments maltose AND glucose; oxidase +; grows on Thayer-Martin (VPN) agar; polysaccharide capsule; LOS endotoxin
diseases: Meningitis, meningococcemia (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome), petechial/purpuric rash
treatment: Ceftriaxone (or penicillin G); rifampin/cipro/ceftriaxone for close-contact prophylaxis
pearl: Waterhouse-Friderichsen: bilateral adrenal hemorrhage + DIC. Asplenic & terminal complement (C5-C9) deficiency at high risk. Vaccine covers capsular serogroups (separate MenB vaccine for serogroup B).

name: Neisseria gonorrhoeae
morphology: Ferments glucose ONLY (not maltose); oxidase +; Thayer-Martin agar; NO polysaccharide capsule; no vaccine; pili & Opa undergo antigenic/phase variation
diseases: Gonorrhea (urethritis/cervicitis), PID, septic arthritis, neonatal conjunctivitis (ophthalmia neonatorum), disseminated gonococcal infection
treatment: Ceftriaxone (add doxycycline if chlamydia not excluded)
pearl: Intracellular GNC within neutrophils on Gram stain. No vaccine (antigenic variation). Causes Fitz-Hugh-Curtis (perihepatitis). Neonatal eye prophylaxis with erythromycin ointment.

name: Haemophilus influenzae
morphology: Requires factor V (NAD+) and factor X (hematin); grows on chocolate agar; satellites around S. aureus on blood agar; type b capsule = polyribosylribitol phosphate (PRP)
diseases: Epiglottitis, meningitis, otitis media, pneumonia, sinusitis (nontypeable strains common in mucosal disease)
treatment: Ceftriaxone (meningitis); amoxicillin +/- clavulanate (mucosal); rifampin prophylaxis for close contacts
pearl: 'Thumbprint sign' on lateral neck x-ray (epiglottitis); cherry-red epiglottis. Hib conjugate vaccine (PRP conjugated to protein). Invasive disease in unvaccinated/asplenic.

name: Haemophilus ducreyi
morphology: 'School of fish'/railroad-track Gram stain arrangement; fastidious; requires factor X (hematin)
diseases: Chancroid: painful genital ulcer with ragged/undermined borders + suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy (buboes)
treatment: Azithromycin or ceftriaxone (single dose); also ciprofloxacin or erythromycin
pearl: 'You DO cry with ducreyi' — PAINFUL ulcer (vs. painless chancre of syphilis). Soft chancre + buboes.

name: Bordetella pertussis
morphology: Bordet-Gengou (potato) agar or Regan-Lowe agar; PCR is diagnostic test of choice; encapsulated
diseases: Whooping cough (pertussis): catarrhal -> paroxysmal (whoop, post-tussive emesis) -> convalescent phases
treatment: Macrolide (azithromycin/erythromycin); supportive care
pearl: Pertussis toxin ADP-ribosylates Gi -> disinhibited adenylate cyclase -> increased cAMP -> lymphocytosis. Inspiratory 'whoop.' Acellular vaccine (DTaP/Tdap). Marked lymphocytosis on CBC.

name: Moraxella catarrhalis
morphology: Oxidase +; DNase +; catalase +; 'hockey puck' colonies slide intact across agar; beta-lactamase producer
diseases: Otitis media, sinusitis, COPD exacerbations, bronchitis/pneumonia in elderly
treatment: Amoxicillin-clavulanate, 2nd/3rd-gen cephalosporins, or macrolides (resistant to plain amoxicillin due to beta-lactamase)
pearl: Top 3 cause of otitis media & sinusitis (with S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae). Common COPD exacerbation pathogen.

name: Brucella spp. (B. melitensis)
morphology: Facultative intracellular; slow-growing aerobe; requires prolonged blood culture; BSL-3
diseases: Brucellosis (undulant fever): cyclic fevers, night sweats, arthralgias, hepatosplenomegaly
treatment: Doxycycline + rifampin (+/- aminoglycoside such as streptomycin/gentamicin)
pearl: Unpasteurized dairy/contact with livestock (goats, cattle). 'Undulant' (rising-falling) fever. Survives in macrophages -> noncaseating granulomas. Zoonosis.

name: Francisella tularensis
morphology: Facultative intracellular; requires cysteine-enriched media; highly infectious (very low inoculum); BSL-3
diseases: Tularemia: ulceroglandular disease (ulcer + regional lymphadenopathy), pneumonic, typhoidal, oculoglandular forms
treatment: Streptomycin or gentamicin (aminoglycosides)
pearl: Ticks (Dermacentor) and rabbits ('rabbit fever'); also deer flies. Caseating granulomas. Potential bioterror agent.

name: Pasteurella multocida
morphology: Bipolar 'safety-pin' staining; oxidase +; catalase +; grows on blood agar; encapsulated
diseases: Cellulitis/wound infection after cat or dog bites; can progress to osteomyelitis/septic arthritis
treatment: Amoxicillin-clavulanate
pearl: Rapidly progressive cellulitis within hours of a cat/dog bite or scratch. Classic 'cat bite' organism.

name: Bartonella henselae
morphology: Fastidious, slow-growing; Warthin-Starry silver stain; facultative intracellular
diseases: Cat-scratch disease (tender regional lymphadenopathy); bacillary angiomatosis in HIV/immunocompromised
treatment: Cat-scratch disease often self-limited (azithromycin if treated); bacillary angiomatosis: doxycycline or erythromycin
pearl: Cat scratch/bite. Bacillary angiomatosis (vascular proliferation) mimics Kaposi sarcoma in AIDS. Warthin-Starry stain.

name: Acinetobacter baumannii
morphology: Strictly aerobic; oxidase NEGATIVE; non-fermenter; catalase +; often multidrug-resistant
diseases: Ventilator-associated/nosocomial pneumonia, bloodstream & wound infections (combat/burn wounds)
treatment: Carbapenems; carbapenem-resistant strains -> colistin/polymyxin, sulbactam (or newer agents)
pearl: Notorious nosocomial MDR/XDR organism in ICUs; 'Iraqibacter' in wounded soldiers. Survives on dry surfaces for long periods.

name: Kingella kingae
morphology: HACEK group member; beta-hemolytic; oxidase +; catalase NEGATIVE; fastidious; improved recovery when inoculated into blood culture bottles
diseases: Pediatric septic arthritis/osteomyelitis (<4 yrs); subacute (HACEK) endocarditis
treatment: Beta-lactams (ceftriaxone / ampicillin)
pearl: HACEK endocarditis (formerly culture-negative/slow-growing) and a leading cause of septic arthritis in young children.

name: Eikenella corrodens
morphology: HACEK group; 'pits/corrodes' agar; bleach-like (hypochlorite) odor; oxidase +; catalase NEGATIVE; facultative anaerobe
diseases: Human bite/clenched-fist wound infections; HACEK endocarditis; abscesses
treatment: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (or ampicillin); resistant to clindamycin, aminoglycosides, and metronidazole
pearl: Classic human-bite ('fight bite'/clenched-fist) pathogen. 'Corrodes' agar; part of HACEK endocarditis group.

name: Escherichia coli
morphology: Lactose-fermenter (pink on MacConkey); green metallic sheen on EMB; indole +; motile; catalase +, oxidase -
diseases: #1 cause of UTI/cystitis & gram-neg sepsis; neonatal meningitis (K1 capsule); traveler's diarrhea (ETEC, LT/ST toxins); HUS (EHEC O157:H7); pneumonia
treatment: 3rd-gen cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, aminoglycosides, nitrofurantoin (cystitis); EHEC supportive only (avoid abx/antimotility - raise HUS risk)
pearl: O157:H7 (EHEC, Shiga-like toxin) = bloody diarrhea + HUS; does NOT ferment sorbitol on sorbitol-MacConkey agar (clear colonies)

name: Salmonella enterica (Typhi & non-typhoidal)
morphology: Non-lactose fermenter; H2S producer (black colonies on TSI/HE/XLD); motile (flagella); acid-labile (large inoculum needed); has Vi capsule (Typhi)
diseases: Typhoid/enteric fever (Typhi); enterocolitis/gastroenteritis (non-typhoidal); osteomyelitis in sickle cell disease
treatment: Ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone for typhoid/invasive disease; gastroenteritis usually supportive (abx prolong fecal carriage)
pearl: Rose spots, pea-soup diarrhea, relative bradycardia; chronic gallbladder carriage (Typhoid Mary); poultry/eggs/turtles/reptiles

name: Shigella (dysenteriae, flexneri, sonnei)
morphology: Non-lactose fermenter; H2S NEGATIVE; NON-motile (no flagella); acid-stable (very low infectious dose)
diseases: Bacillary dysentery (bloody, mucoid diarrhea with fever, tenesmus); Shiga toxin -> HUS (esp. S. dysenteriae type 1)
treatment: Fluoroquinolone or ceftriaxone (azithromycin alternative); avoid antimotility agents
pearl: Lowest infectious dose (~10-100 organisms); invades M cells of Peyer patches, cell-to-cell spread via actin polymerization; daycare/fecal-oral

name: Klebsiella pneumoniae
morphology: Lactose-fermenter (mucoid pink colonies); large polysaccharide capsule; non-motile; urease +; indole -
diseases: Lobar pneumonia (alcoholics/aspiration); UTI; nosocomial sepsis; liver abscess; major carbapenem-resistant organism (KPC, CRE)
treatment: Carbapenem for ESBL strains; 3rd-gen cephalosporin if susceptible; CRE -> ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, or colistin
pearl: Currant-jelly (thick, bloody) sputum; positive mucoid string test in hypervirulent strains; 4 A's: Aspiration, Alcoholics, Abscess, diAbetes

name: Proteus mirabilis
morphology: Swarming motility (concentric bull's-eye waves) on agar; strongly urease +; non-lactose fermenter; H2S +; fishy/burnt-chocolate odor
diseases: UTI (esp. with struvite/staghorn stones); catheter-associated UTI; pyelonephritis
treatment: TMP-SMX or fluoroquinolone; ampicillin or 3rd-gen cephalosporin if susceptible (intrinsically nitrofurantoin-resistant)
pearl: Urease splits urea -> alkaline urine -> struvite (magnesium-ammonium-phosphate) staghorn calculi; OX antigens cross-react with Rickettsia (Weil-Felix)

name: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
morphology: Oxidase POSITIVE; non-lactose fermenter; blue-green pigment (pyocyanin + yellow-green pyoverdine); grape/corn-tortilla odor; obligate aerobe
diseases: Nosocomial PNA; CF & ventilator pneumonia; burn wound sepsis; otitis externa/malignant OE (diabetics); hot-tub folliculitis; ecthyma gangrenosum; osteomyelitis (IVDU, diabetic foot)
treatment: Antipseudomonal beta-lactam (pip-tazo, cefepime, ceftazidime, meropenem) +/- antipseudomonal aminoglycoside or fluoroquinolone (cipro/levo)
pearl: Exotoxin A inhibits EF-2 (like diphtheria toxin); ecthyma gangrenosum in neutropenics; thrives in water/biofilms; classic nosocomial workhorse

name: Enterobacter (cloacae / Klebsiella aerogenes)
morphology: Lactose-fermenter; motile; inducible chromosomal AmpC beta-lactamase; oxidase -
diseases: Nosocomial UTI, pneumonia, bacteremia, wound/device infections
treatment: Cefepime or carbapenem (avoid 3rd-gen cephalosporins -> AmpC derepression/resistance); fluoroquinolone alternative
pearl: SPACE/SPICE organism with inducible AmpC; resistance can emerge during 3rd-gen cephalosporin therapy; E. aerogenes reclassified as Klebsiella aerogenes

name: Serratia marcescens
morphology: Non-lactose fermenter (slow/late); red pigment (prodigiosin) at room temp ~25C; DNase +; gelatinase/lipase +; motile
diseases: Nosocomial UTI, pneumonia, wound/line infections; right-sided endocarditis in IV drug users
treatment: Carbapenem or cefepime (inducible AmpC, often multidrug-resistant); fluoroquinolone or aminoglycoside per susceptibility
pearl: Red-pigmented colonies (prodigiosin); SPACE/SPICE member with inducible AmpC; classic in catheters & IV drug users

name: Yersinia enterocolitica
morphology: Non-lactose fermenter; grows at cold temps (4C, cold enrichment); bipolar (safety-pin) staining; urease +; motile at 25C, non-motile at 37C
diseases: Enterocolitis (esp. young children); mesenteric adenitis mimicking appendicitis (pseudoappendicitis); fever, bloody diarrhea; reactive arthritis
treatment: Usually self-limited; severe/systemic -> fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX or 3rd-gen cephalosporin +/- aminoglycoside
pearl: Pseudoappendicitis in kids; transmitted via pork (chitterlings), unpasteurized milk, puppy/pet feces; cold growth (contaminated refrigerated blood transfusions)

name: Vibrio cholerae
morphology: Oxidase POSITIVE; comma/curved rod; grows on TCBS agar (yellow colonies from sucrose fermentation); darting/shooting-star motility; alkaline-tolerant, acid-labile
diseases: Cholera -- profuse watery 'rice-water stool', massive secretory diarrhea, rapid dehydration
treatment: Aggressive oral/IV rehydration is the mainstay; doxycycline or azithromycin to shorten duration & shedding
pearl: Cholera toxin (A-B subunit; ADP-ribosylates Gs -> permanent up cAMP -> Cl-/H2O efflux); rice-water stools; contaminated water/undercooked shellfish

name: Vibrio parahaemolyticus / vulnificus
type: Gram-negative curved rod (oxidase +, halophilic, facultative anaerobe)
morphology: Oxidase +; comma-shaped; salt-loving (halophilic); grows on TCBS (parahaemolyticus green/non-sucrose, vulnificus blue-green)
diseases: parahaemolyticus: seafood gastroenteritis (watery diarrhea). vulnificus: wound infection/cellulitis & fulminant sepsis (esp. cirrhosis, hemochromatosis/iron overload)
treatment: Doxycycline + ceftriaxone (vulnificus sepsis/severe wound); supportive rehydration for gastroenteritis
pearl: Raw oysters/shellfish & seawater wound exposure; V. vulnificus -> hemorrhagic bullae & sepsis in iron-overload/chronic liver disease

name: Campylobacter jejuni
morphology: Oxidase +; comma/S-shaped (seagull-wing) rods; grows at 42C on Skirrow/Campy agar, microaerophilic; motile (darting, single polar flagellum)
diseases: Most common bacterial cause of gastroenteritis -- bloody diarrhea, cramps, fever; antecedent of Guillain-Barre syndrome & reactive arthritis
treatment: Usually supportive; azithromycin (first-line if abx needed) or fluoroquinolone (rising resistance) for severe disease
pearl: Comma/seagull-shaped; grows at 42C; #1 antecedent of Guillain-Barre (molecular mimicry of gangliosides) & reactive arthritis; from poultry, unpasteurized milk, puppies

name: Helicobacter pylori
morphology: Strongly urease POSITIVE (basis of urea breath test); curved/spiral; highly motile (multiple polar flagella); oxidase + and catalase +
diseases: Chronic gastritis, peptic & duodenal ulcers; risk factor for gastric adenocarcinoma & gastric MALT lymphoma
treatment: Triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin; OR bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline) where clarithromycin resistance is high
pearl: Urease generates ammonia cloud buffering gastric acid; risk factor for gastric cancer & MALToma; diagnose via urea breath test or stool antigen

name: Vibrio cholerae
morphology: Oxidase +; grows on alkaline TCBS agar (yellow colonies, sucrose fermenter); single polar (monotrichous) flagellum; rapid darting "shooting star" motility
diseases: Cholera: profuse watery "rice-water" stool diarrhea, rapid dehydration, hypovolemic shock
treatment: Prompt oral/IV rehydration (mainstay); doxycycline or azithromycin shortens duration/shedding
pearl: Rice-water stools; cholera toxin permanently activates Gs (ADP-ribosylation) -> rising cAMP -> Cl-/H2O efflux into gut lumen; acid-labile (large inoculum needed)

name: Vibrio parahaemolyticus
morphology: Oxidase +; salt-loving; TCBS green colonies (sucrose non-fermenter); Kanagawa phenomenon = beta-hemolysis on Wagatsuma agar from thermostable direct hemolysin (TDH)
diseases: Acute watery (sometimes bloody) self-limited gastroenteritis from raw/undercooked seafood (esp. shellfish/oysters)
treatment: Supportive rehydration; doxycycline or fluoroquinolone if severe/prolonged
pearl: Leading cause of seafood-associated gastroenteritis (raw oysters); halophilic marine Vibrio; TDH (Kanagawa+) marks pathogenic strains

name: Vibrio vulnificus
morphology: Oxidase +; salt-loving marine organism; lactose fermenter (key feature distinguishing it from most other vibrios)
diseases: Rapidly progressive necrotizing fasciitis/cellulitis (seawater wound exposure); fulminant sepsis with hemorrhagic bullae after raw oyster ingestion
treatment: Doxycycline + 3rd-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone/ceftazidime); urgent surgical debridement
pearl: Deadly in cirrhotics/hemochromatosis (iron overload promotes growth); hemorrhagic bullae + sepsis after raw oysters or seawater wound

name: Campylobacter jejuni
morphology: Oxidase +, catalase +; microaerophilic; grows at 42C on Skirrow/Campy (CVA) agar; corkscrew/darting motility
diseases: Bloody/inflammatory diarrhea with cramping abdominal pain and fever (most common bacterial enteritis in US)
treatment: Usually self-limited/supportive; azithromycin (or erythromycin) first-line if severe; fluoroquinolone (rising resistance)
pearl: #1 antecedent of Guillain-Barre (molecular mimicry: LOS vs gangliosides) & reactive arthritis; seagull-shaped; from poultry, unpasteurized milk, puppies; thermophilic at 42C

name: Helicobacter pylori
morphology: Oxidase +, catalase +, strongly urease +; microaerophilic; multiple sheathed polar (lophotrichous) flagella
diseases: Chronic (antral) gastritis, peptic ulcers (esp. duodenal); risk for gastric adenocarcinoma & MALT lymphoma
treatment: Quadruple therapy now preferred: PPI + bismuth + tetracycline + metronidazole; or PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin where clarithromycin resistance is low
pearl: Urease + (urea breath/stool antigen tests; ammonia buffers acid for colonization); class I carcinogen for gastric cancer & MALT lymphoma; curved rod in gastric antrum

name: Bacteroides fragilis
morphology: Obligate anaerobe; non-spore-forming; bile-resistant (grows in 20% bile); LPS lipid A has reduced endotoxin potency; antiphagocytic polysaccharide capsule
diseases: Intra-abdominal & pelvic abscesses, peritonitis (post-surgery/perforation); polymicrobial infections below the diaphragm
treatment: Metronidazole; carbapenems or piperacillin-tazobactam (commonly beta-lactamase producer)
pearl: Most common anaerobe in abscesses below the diaphragm; major colonic flora; resistant to aminoglycosides (O2-dependent uptake fails anaerobically)

name: Campylobacter fetus
morphology: Oxidase +; microaerophilic; grows at 25C and 37C but NOT at 42C (opposite of C. jejuni); S-layer surface protein
diseases: Systemic infection - bacteremia, endovascular/vascular infections, septic abortion, meningitis (esp. elderly/immunocompromised)
treatment: Systemic disease: ampicillin or a carbapenem; add aminoglycoside (e.g., gentamicin) for bacteremia/endovascular infection
pearl: Spreads systemically (vascular tropism) rather than causing diarrhea; capsule-like S-layer protein resists complement/phagocytosis; fails to grow at 42C

name: Prevotella / Porphyromonas spp.
morphology: Obligate anaerobes; oral/genital flora; foul-smelling (short-chain fatty acid) pus; produce black-pigmented colonies
diseases: Oral/dental abscesses, aspiration pneumonia & lung abscess, periodontitis, head & neck infections
treatment: Metronidazole, clindamycin, or beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor (amox-clav, pip-tazo)
pearl: Foul-smelling anaerobes ABOVE the diaphragm (oral/respiratory) vs B. fragilis below; aspiration pneumonia/lung abscess in alcoholics with poor dentition

name: Fusobacterium necrophorum
type: Gram-negative anaerobic rod (long, slender, spindle/pointed ends)
morphology: Obligate anaerobe; pleomorphic filamentous rods with tapered ends; oropharyngeal flora
diseases: Lemierre syndrome (septic thrombophlebitis of internal jugular vein after pharyngitis with septic pulmonary emboli); periodontal/aspiration infections, abscesses
treatment: Beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor or metronidazole (+/- carbapenem); anticoagulation controversial/case-by-case
pearl: Lemierre syndrome: post-pharyngitis sepsis -> internal jugular vein thrombosis + septic pulmonary emboli in a young, previously healthy adult

name: Vibrio alginolyticus
morphology: Oxidase +; halophilic (highly salt-tolerant); TCBS yellow colonies (sucrose fermenter)
diseases: Otitis externa/media and wound/soft-tissue infections after marine or recreational saltwater exposure
treatment: Doxycycline +/- ceftazidime; wound care/debridement as needed
pearl: Marine Vibrio causing ear and wound infections in swimmers/fishermen after saltwater exposure

name: Brucella (B. melitensis, abortus, suis)
morphology: Tiny GNC, slow-growing, urease+, oxidase+, catalase+; survives in macrophages; prolonged blood/bone-marrow culture (alert lab - biosafety hazard)
diseases: Brucellosis: undulant (cyclic) fever, night sweats, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly; vertebral osteomyelitis (sacroiliitis)
treatment: Doxycycline + rifampin (or doxy + streptomycin/gentamicin) x6 weeks
pearl: Undulant fever from unpasteurized dairy/goat cheese; affects farmers, vets, abattoir workers. Macrophage survival = relapse/chronicity

name: Francisella tularensis
morphology: Tiny pleomorphic GNC; needs cysteine-enriched media (e.g. cysteine-glucose-blood/chocolate); highly infectious (<10 organisms); Category A bioterror; dx by serology (culture hazardous)
diseases: Tularemia: ulceroglandular (ulcer + regional lymphadenopathy), pneumonic, typhoidal, oculoglandular
treatment: Streptomycin (or gentamicin); doxy/cipro alternatives
pearl: Rabbits/ticks (Dermacentor) + Martha's Vineyard; 'rabbit/deerfly fever.' Forms caseating granulomas mimicking TB

name: Yersinia pestis
morphology: Bipolar 'safety pin' staining (Wright/Giemsa/Wayson); facultative anaerobe; F1 capsule; Yop virulence factors (type III secretion)
diseases: Plague: bubonic (painful bubo), septicemic (DIC, acral gangrene = 'black death'), pneumonic (person-to-person, fatal)
treatment: Streptomycin or gentamicin; doxycycline/cipro alternatives
pearl: Fleas (Xenopsylla) from prairie dogs/rodents; Southwest US. 'Safety pin' bipolar staining; buboes

name: Yersinia enterocolitica
morphology: Motile at 25C (not 37C); grows at 4C (cold enrichment); bipolar staining; CIN agar (bull's-eye colonies)
diseases: Enterocolitis (bloody diarrhea); pseudoappendicitis (mesenteric adenitis/terminal ileitis); reactive arthritis
treatment: Usually self-limited; TMP-SMX or fluoroquinolone if severe
pearl: Pork (chitterlings), contaminated milk; pet feces. Mimics appendicitis in kids; iron overload (hemochromatosis/transfusion) predisposes

name: Bartonella henselae
morphology: Slow-growing, fastidious; Warthin-Starry silver stain; oxidase-negative; dx mainly by serology/PCR
diseases: Cat-scratch disease (regional lymphadenopathy + papule); bacillary angiomatosis & peliosis hepatis in HIV/AIDS
treatment: Cat-scratch: azithromycin; bacillary angiomatosis: doxycycline or erythromycin
pearl: Kittens/cat scratch. Bacillary angiomatosis (vascular tumors) mimics Kaposi sarcoma in HIV - distinguish on biopsy

name: Bartonella quintana
morphology: Fastidious; blood culture/serology; Warthin-Starry stain on tissue
diseases: Trench fever (relapsing 5-day fever, shin pain); bacillary angiomatosis; culture-negative endocarditis
treatment: Doxycycline +/- gentamicin (endocarditis); erythromycin/doxy for angiomatosis
pearl: Body louse (Pediculus) - homeless/wartime; 'quintana' = quintan (5-day) fever cycle

name: Pasteurella multocida
morphology: Bipolar staining GNC; catalase+, oxidase+, indole+; grows on blood agar but NOT MacConkey; mousy/musty odor
diseases: Rapidly progressive cellulitis after cat/dog bites; lymphangitis; osteomyelitis; abscess
treatment: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (penicillin-sensitive)
pearl: Cat/dog bites/scratches - cellulitis within hours (faster than staph/strep). Penicillin-susceptible (unusual for GNR)

name: Coxiella burnetii
type: Obligate intracellular gram-negative (Coxiella/Legionellales; formerly grouped with Rickettsia)
morphology: Does not stain well on Gram; spore-like form (environmentally stable); phase I/II antigen serology; no vector needed
diseases: Q fever: atypical pneumonia, granulomatous hepatitis, flu-like illness; chronic = culture-negative endocarditis
treatment: Acute: doxycycline; chronic/endocarditis: doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine (months to years)
pearl: Inhaled aerosols from cattle/sheep/goat birth products (placenta); NO arthropod vector; 'Q' = query fever

name: Capnocytophaga canimorsus
morphology: Thin fusiform GNR; requires CO2 (capnophilic); slow-growing; oxidase+, catalase+
diseases: Fulminant sepsis/DIC after dog bites, esp. asplenic, cirrhotic, or alcoholic patients; meningitis
treatment: Amoxicillin-clavulanate, penicillin G, or a beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor; carbapenem for severe sepsis
pearl: Dog bites in ASPLENIC patients - overwhelming sepsis with purpura fulminans; classic post-splenectomy killer

name: Leptospira interrogans
morphology: Thin tightly-coiled spirochete with hooked ends; seen on dark-field microscopy; EMJH culture (slow); serology (MAT)
diseases: Leptospirosis: flu-like + conjunctival suffusion; Weil disease (jaundice, AKI, hemorrhage)
treatment: Doxycycline (mild) or penicillin G/ceftriaxone (severe)
pearl: Rat urine in fresh water; surfers/triathletes/Hawaii. Conjunctival suffusion + jaundice (Weil's)

name: Borrelia burgdorferi
type: Spirochete (zoonotic); stains with Giemsa, poorly with Gram
morphology: Large loose-coiled spirochete; Giemsa/dark-field; serology (ELISA -> Western blot, 2-tier)
diseases: Lyme disease: erythema migrans (target rash); later - facial palsy, AV block (carditis), arthritis, neuroborreliosis
treatment: Doxycycline (early); ceftriaxone for CNS/cardiac disease
pearl: Ixodes tick (mice/deer reservoir), Northeast US; bull's-eye/target erythema migrans rash

name: Bacillus anthracis (zoonotic GNR mimic)
type: Gram-POSITIVE spore-forming rod (zoonotic, included for contrast)
morphology: Large boxcar GPR in chains; polypeptide (poly-D-glutamate) capsule; 'Medusa head' colonies; nonhemolytic; spores
diseases: Cutaneous anthrax (painless black eschar); inhalation (woolsorter's, mediastinal widening); GI
treatment: Ciprofloxacin or doxycycline (+ raxibacumab/antitoxin for systemic)
pearl: Hides/wool/livestock; bioterror. Painless black eschar; toxin = protective antigen + edema factor (adenylate cyclase) + lethal factor

name: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae
morphology: Thin pleomorphic GPR; H2S+ on TSI; nonmotile; alpha-hemolytic; catalase-negative
diseases: Erysipeloid: violaceous, well-demarcated hand cellulitis (no suppuration); rarely endocarditis
treatment: Penicillin (intrinsically resistant to vancomycin)
pearl: Fish handlers, butchers, pig farmers; 'seal finger.' Vancomycin-RESISTANT - key board distractor

name: Mycoplasma pneumoniae
type: No cell wall (not Gram-stainable); smallest self-replicating/free-living organism
morphology: No peptidoglycan; sterol (cholesterol)-containing membrane; 'fried-egg' colonies on Eaton agar; cold agglutinins (IgM); PCR is now test of choice (slow to culture)
diseases: Atypical 'walking' pneumonia in young adults/military/dorms; bullous myringitis; extrapulmonary: Mycoplasma-induced rash & mucositis (MIRM/SJS-like), cold-agglutinin hemolytic anemia
treatment: Macrolide (azithromycin) or doxycycline (or respiratory fluoroquinolone); NO beta-lactams (no cell wall target)
pearl: Walking pneumonia in teens/military barracks; CXR looks worse than exam; IgM cold agglutinins cause cold-agglutinin hemolysis

name: Chlamydia trachomatis
type: Obligate intracellular; Gram-negative-like but lacks classic muramic acid/peptidoglycan sacculus ('chlamydial anomaly'); biphasic EB/RB cycle
morphology: Cytoplasmic inclusions on Giemsa/iodine; NAAT is test of choice; 'energy parasite' (uses host ATP)
diseases: Serovars A-C: trachoma (leading infectious cause of blindness); D-K: urethritis/cervicitis, PID, neonatal conjunctivitis & pneumonia; L1-L3: lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV)
treatment: Azithromycin single dose or doxycycline 7 days; treat partners; co-treat empirically for gonorrhea
pearl: Elementary body = infectious/Enters; Reticulate body = Replicates. #1 reportable bacterial STI; reactive arthritis (can't see/pee/climb a tree)

name: Chlamydophila pneumoniae
type: Obligate intracellular; biphasic EB/RB cycle; lacks classic peptidoglycan
morphology: Cytoplasmic inclusions; diagnosed mainly by serology/PCR; uses host ATP
diseases: Atypical community-acquired pneumonia (often mild, prolonged cough); bronchitis; pharyngitis/sinusitis
treatment: Macrolide or doxycycline (respiratory fluoroquinolone alternative)
pearl: Atypical pneumonia spread person-to-person via respiratory droplets; hoarseness/sore throat often precedes the cough

name: Chlamydophila psittaci
type: Obligate intracellular; biphasic EB/RB cycle; lacks classic peptidoglycan
morphology: Cytoplasmic inclusions; serology/PCR; avian reservoir (zoonotic, no person-to-person)
diseases: Psittacosis: atypical pneumonia + high fever, severe headache, often hepatosplenomegaly; can cause severe systemic illness
treatment: Doxycycline (first-line)
pearl: Bird/parrot exposure (pet stores, poultry/turkey processing); 'parrot fever'

name: Rickettsia rickettsii
morphology: Hard to Gram-stain; Giemsa; diagnosis by serology/PCR/skin biopsy IHC; Weil-Felix (historical, spotted fever group = OX-19 AND OX-2 +); Dermacentor tick vector
diseases: Rocky Mountain spotted fever: fever, headache, then rash starting on wrists/ankles (centripetal) spreading to trunk, palms & soles; can progress to vasculitis/multiorgan failure
treatment: Doxycycline (drug of choice even in children & pregnancy; start empirically, don't wait for labs)
pearl: Rash on palms/soles spreading inward; most cases in southeastern/south-central US (e.g., the Carolinas), not the Rockies; endothelial invasion -> vasculitis

name: Rickettsia prowazekii
morphology: Stains poorly; serology/PCR; Weil-Felix OX-19 + (typhus group); human body louse (Pediculus) vector; flying squirrel reservoir (US)
diseases: Epidemic typhus: fever, headache, centrifugal rash (trunk -> outward) sparing palms/soles/face; Brill-Zinsser = late recrudescence
treatment: Doxycycline (chloramphenicol alternative)
pearl: Body louse in war/refugee/crowding settings; rash starts on trunk and spreads outward, sparing palms & soles (opposite of RMSF)

name: Rickettsia typhi
morphology: Stains poorly; serology/PCR; Weil-Felix OX-19 + (typhus group); rat flea (Xenopsylla) vector, rat/opossum reservoir
diseases: Endemic (murine) typhus: milder fever, headache, central rash sparing palms/soles
treatment: Doxycycline
pearl: Rat/cat flea; endemic along the Gulf Coast/Texas & southern California; milder course than epidemic typhus

name: Orientia tsutsugamushi
type: Obligate intracellular Gram-negative (former Rickettsia; lacks LPS & peptidoglycan)
morphology: Stains poorly; serology/PCR; Weil-Felix OX-K + (only OX-K reactive); chigger (larval trombiculid mite) vector
diseases: Scrub typhus: fever, eschar at bite site, regional lymphadenopathy, rash; can cause pneumonitis/meningoencephalitis
treatment: Doxycycline (azithromycin alternative, including in pregnancy)
pearl: Chigger bite -> eschar; confined to the 'tsutsugamushi triangle' of SE Asia/Asia-Pacific

name: Coxiella burnetii
type: Obligate intracellular Gram-negative; forms a highly resistant spore-like small-cell variant (not a true endospore)
morphology: Stains poorly on Gram; Gimenez stain; phase variation (phase I virulent / phase II); diagnosis by serology (phase I vs II IgG); replicates in acidified phagolysosome; environmentally stable
diseases: Q fever: acute = atypical pneumonia, granulomatous hepatitis, flu-like illness; chronic = culture-negative endocarditis (high phase I IgG)
treatment: Doxycycline (acute); doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine, prolonged (chronic/endocarditis)
pearl: Inhaled from cattle/sheep/goat birth fluids, placenta & dust; NO arthropod vector and NO rash (unlike Rickettsia); very low infectious dose

name: Legionella pneumophila
type: Gram-negative rod (stains poorly with standard Gram)
morphology: Silver stain (Dieterle); grows on buffered charcoal yeast extract (BCYE) agar with cysteine + iron; urine antigen test detects serogroup 1 ONLY; facultative intracellular in alveolar macrophages
diseases: Legionnaires' disease: severe pneumonia + high fever, hyponatremia, diarrhea, elevated LFTs, confusion; Pontiac fever (self-limited flu-like, no pneumonia)
treatment: Respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin) or macrolide (azithromycin)
pearl: Aerosolized water (cooling towers, AC, hot tubs); smokers/elderly/immunocompromised; hyponatremia + diarrhea + relative bradycardia; no person-to-person spread

name: Anaplasma phagocytophilum
morphology: Morulae in granulocytes/neutrophils on blood smear; diagnosis by PCR/serology; Ixodes tick vector
diseases: Human granulocytic anaplasmosis: fever, headache, myalgia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, elevated LFTs (rash rare)
treatment: Doxycycline
pearl: Ixodes tick (same as Lyme/Babesia) -> consider co-infection; morulae in neutrophils; Upper Midwest/Northeast US

name: Ehrlichia chaffeensis
morphology: Morulae in monocytes/macrophages; diagnosis by PCR/serology; Lone Star tick (Amblyomma americanum) vector
diseases: Human monocytic ehrlichiosis: fever, headache, myalgia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, elevated LFTs; rash uncommon ('spotless')
treatment: Doxycycline
pearl: Morulae in monocytes; Lone Star tick, southeastern/south-central US; 'spotless' RMSF-like illness

name: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
type: Acid-fast bacillus (weakly gram+, obligate aerobe, non-motile rod)
morphology: Acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen/auramine-rhodamine); mycolic acid-rich wall; serpentine 'cord factor' (trehalose dimycolate) cords; slow growth (~3-6 wk) on Lowenstein-Jensen (buff, rough, tough colonies); niacin+, catalase neg at 68C
diseases: Pulmonary TB (apical/cavitary 2ndary); Ghon/miliary & extrapulmonary; Pott disease (vertebral); scrofula (cervical lymphadenitis); TB meningitis; renal/adrenal (Addison)
treatment: RIPE x2 mo: Rifampin, Isoniazid (+pyridoxine B6), Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol; then Rifampin+Isoniazid x4 mo (total 6 mo)
pearl: Ghon complex (calcified focus + hilar node); caseating granuloma w/ Langhans giant cells; PPD/IGRA+; reactivates in apices (high O2 tension)

name: Mycobacterium leprae
type: Acid-fast bacillus (obligate intracellular; cannot be cultured in vitro)
morphology: Unculturable in vitro; grows in armadillos & mouse footpad; prefers cool temps (~30C, skin/extremities/nerves); acid-fast bacilli in skin/nerve biopsy (Schwann cell tropism)
diseases: Leprosy (Hansen disease): tuberculoid (paucibacillary, strong Th1/cell-mediated, few well-defined hypopigmented lesions) vs lepromatous (multibacillary, Th2/humoral, diffuse, communicable)
treatment: Tuberculoid (paucibacillary): Dapsone + Rifampin x6 mo. Lepromatous (multibacillary): Dapsone + Rifampin + Clofazimine x12 mo
pearl: Lepromatous = 'leonine facies', symmetric anesthetic lesions, glove-and-stocking sensory loss, madarosis (loss of lateral eyebrows); armadillo reservoir; cool-temp tropism

name: Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare (MAC)
morphology: Acid-fast; slow-growing ubiquitous environmental mycobacteria (soil/water); causes disseminated disease in advanced AIDS
diseases: Disseminated infection (fever, night sweats, weight loss, diarrhea, hepatosplenomegaly) in AIDS CD4<50; chronic pulmonary disease in COPD/elderly & bronchiectasis (Lady Windermere syndrome)
treatment: Macrolide (azithromycin or clarithromycin) + ethambutol +/- rifabutin; azithromycin or clarithromycin prophylaxis if CD4<50
pearl: Most common NTM causing disseminated disease in AIDS; prophylaxis with azithromycin when CD4<50; rifabutin preferred over rifampin (less CYP induction with antiretrovirals)

name: Treponema pallidum
morphology: Too thin to see on light microscopy/Gram stain; visualized by darkfield microscopy or direct fluorescent antibody (DFA); cannot be cultured in vitro
diseases: Syphilis: 1deg painless chancre; 2deg maculopapular rash (incl. palms/soles) + condylomata lata + lymphadenopathy; 3deg gummas, ascending aortitis (vasa vasorum), neurosyphilis/tabes dorsalis; congenital syphilis
treatment: Penicillin G (benzathine IM for early; IV aqueous penicillin G for neurosyphilis); penicillin-desensitize if pregnant & allergic
pearl: Screen with nontreponemal RPR/VDRL -> confirm with treponemal FTA-ABS/TPPA; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction after tx; Argyll Robertson pupil (accommodates but does NOT react to light); VDRL false positives (Viruses, Drugs, Rheumatic fever/RA, Lupus/Leprosy)

name: Borrelia burgdorferi
morphology: Large spirochete; visible on Giemsa/Wright stain; diagnosis is clinical + serology (ELISA screen -> confirmatory Western blot/immunoblot)
diseases: Lyme disease: early localized = erythema migrans + flu-like; early disseminated = carditis (transient AV block), bilateral facial (Bell) palsy, migratory arthralgias; late = monoarticular/oligoarticular arthritis, encephalopathy
treatment: Doxycycline (early/localized); ceftriaxone (or cefotaxime) for carditis/CNS/disseminated; amoxicillin or cefuroxime in kids/pregnancy
pearl: 'Bull's-eye'/target erythema migrans; Ixodes (deer) tick vector (also transmits Anaplasma & Babesia); reservoir = white-footed mouse, deer required for tick life cycle; bilateral Bell palsy is classic

name: Borrelia recurrentis / hermsii
morphology: Large spirochete directly visible on peripheral blood smear (Giemsa/Wright) during febrile spikes
diseases: Relapsing fever: recurrent fever spikes from antigenic variation of surface (Vmp) lipoproteins, each relapse from a new antigenic variant
treatment: Doxycycline, tetracycline, or erythromycin (anticipate Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction, can be severe)
pearl: B. recurrentis = louse-borne (epidemic, human-to-human via Pediculus, no animal reservoir); B. hermsii = soft (Ornithodoros) tick-borne (endemic); relapses due to antigenic variation

name: Leptospira interrogans
type: Spirochete (thin, tightly coiled, with hooked/question-mark ends; obligate aerobe)
morphology: Tightly coiled spirochete with hooked ends ('question mark' shape); darkfield microscopy; diagnosis by serology (microscopic agglutination test, MAT) or PCR; culture in EMJH media (slow)
diseases: Leptospirosis (biphasic: septicemic then immune phase); severe form = Weil disease (jaundice, acute kidney injury/azotemia, hemorrhage); conjunctival suffusion
treatment: Penicillin G or doxycycline (IV ceftriaxone/penicillin for severe; doxycycline for prophylaxis)
pearl: Acquired via water contaminated with animal (rodent) urine; surfers/tropics/Hawaii, classic flu-like illness + conjunctival suffusion + severe calf/myalgia

name: Mycobacterium kansasii
morphology: Acid-fast; photochromogen (produces yellow-orange pigment only after light exposure); slow-growing; long beaded/banded bacilli
diseases: Chronic cavitary pulmonary disease clinically/radiographically resembling TB (often in COPD/middle-aged men); occasionally disseminated in immunocompromised
treatment: Rifampin + isoniazid + ethambutol (typically prolonged, ~12 mo after culture conversion)
pearl: Classic TB mimic in the lungs; photochromogen = pigment develops only with light exposure

name: Mycobacterium marinum
morphology: Acid-fast; slow-growing; grows best at cooler temperatures (~30-32C), favoring skin of extremities
diseases: Cutaneous granulomas ('fish tank'/'swimming pool' granuloma); nodular (sporotrichoid) lymphangitis ascending the hand/arm
treatment: Clarithromycin (often combined with ethambutol or rifampin)
pearl: 'Fish tank/aquarium granuloma' in aquarium handlers & fishermen; cool-temperature tropism (extremities)

name: Mycobacterium scrofulaceum
morphology: Acid-fast; scotochromogen (yellow-orange pigment in both dark and light); slow-growing
diseases: Cervical lymphadenitis (scrofula) in young children
treatment: Surgical excision of affected node(s) is first-line; antimycobacterials if extensive/recurrent
pearl: Named NTM cause of pediatric cervical lymphadenitis (though MAC is now the most common NTM cause overall); surgery preferred over drugs

name: Mycobacterium abscessus / fortuitum / chelonae (rapid growers)
morphology: Acid-fast; rapid growers forming visible colonies in <7 days on subculture
diseases: Skin/soft-tissue, catheter & surgical/cosmetic wound infections; chronic pulmonary disease (esp. cystic fibrosis & bronchiectasis)
treatment: Macrolide-based multidrug (e.g., azithromycin/clarithromycin) + amikacin +/- cefoxitin/imipenem/tigecycline; M. abscessus is highly drug-resistant
pearl: Post-procedure (cosmetic surgery, tattoo, injection, pedicure) wound infections; M. abscessus is notoriously multidrug-resistant

name: Treponema pallidum subsp. pertenue (& endemic treponematoses)
type: Spirochete (nonvenereal Treponema, morphologically identical to T. pallidum)
morphology: Morphologically & serologically indistinguishable from syphilis; darkfield; cross-reacts on RPR/VDRL & FTA-ABS; unculturable
diseases: Yaws (subsp. pertenue, skin/bone, tropical); pinta (T. carateum, skin pigment changes); bejel/endemic syphilis (subsp. endemicum, oral/skin) - nonvenereal, spread by skin contact, mostly children
treatment: Penicillin (single-dose benzathine penicillin G); single-dose oral azithromycin used in WHO mass-treatment campaigns
pearl: Nonvenereal 'cousins' of syphilis; produce positive syphilis serology without sexual transmission; affect children in tropical/poor-hygiene settings

name: Herpes simplex virus 1 (HSV-1)
morphology: Tzanck smear: multinucleated giant cells; Cowdry A intranuclear inclusions. PCR most sensitive (esp. CSF in encephalitis). Latent in trigeminal ganglia.
diseases: Gingivostomatitis, herpes labialis (cold sores), keratoconjunctivitis, temporal lobe encephalitis, herpetic whitlow, esophagitis.
treatment: Acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir; IV acyclovir for encephalitis; foscarnet if resistant.
pearl: Most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis in US; hemorrhagic temporal lobe necrosis. Dendritic corneal ulcers (avoid steroids alone).

name: Herpes simplex virus 2 (HSV-2)
morphology: Tzanck smear: multinucleated giant cells, Cowdry A inclusions. Latent in sacral (dorsal root) ganglia. PCR confirms.
diseases: Genital herpes (painful vesicles/ulcers), neonatal herpes, aseptic (Mollaret) recurrent meningitis.
treatment: Acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir; IV acyclovir for neonatal/disseminated disease.
pearl: Neonatal transmission via vaginal delivery; C-section if active lesions. Most common cause of genital ulcers in US.

name: Varicella-zoster virus (VZV / HHV-3)
morphology: Tzanck smear: multinucleated giant cells, Cowdry A inclusions. Latent in dorsal root/trigeminal ganglia.
diseases: Chickenpox (varicella); shingles (zoster) in dermatomal distribution; post-herpetic neuralgia; encephalitis, pneumonia (esp. adults).
treatment: Acyclovir/valacyclovir/famciclovir; live attenuated vaccine (varicella) & recombinant Shingrix (zoster).
pearl: 'Dewdrops on a rose petal'; lesions in all stages. Ramsay Hunt (CN VII + ear); Hutchinson sign (nose tip = nasociliary/ocular involvement).

name: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV / HHV-4)
morphology: Atypical (Downey) lymphocytes = reactive CD8+ T cells; positive Monospot (heterophile antibodies). Infects B cells via CD21 (CR2).
diseases: Infectious mononucleosis; Burkitt & Hodgkin lymphoma; nasopharyngeal carcinoma; CNS lymphoma in AIDS; oral hairy leukoplakia; PTLD.
treatment: Supportive (self-limited); avoid contact sports ~3-4 wks (splenic rupture). Avoid ampicillin/amoxicillin (morbilliform rash).
pearl: Triad: fever, pharyngitis (exudative), posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Burkitt = 'starry sky', t(8;14) c-myc.

name: Cytomegalovirus (CMV / HHV-5)
morphology: 'Owl's eye' intranuclear inclusions; cytomegaly. Latent in mononuclear cells. PCR for viral load; Monospot-negative.
diseases: Congenital CMV (#1 congenital infection); heterophile-negative mononucleosis; retinitis, colitis, esophagitis, pneumonitis in AIDS/transplant.
treatment: Ganciclovir, valganciclovir; foscarnet or cidofovir if resistant.
pearl: Owl's eye inclusions. Congenital: periventricular calcifications, sensorineural deafness, 'blueberry muffin' rash. Retinitis when CD4 <50.

name: Human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6)
morphology: Infects CD4+ T cells (uses CD46); largely clinical diagnosis. Latent, reactivates in transplant patients. HHV-6B causes most childhood disease.
diseases: Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum / sixth disease); febrile seizures; encephalitis in immunocompromised/transplant.
treatment: Supportive; ganciclovir/foscarnet for severe disease in immunocompromised.
pearl: High fever for several days, then rash appears AFTER fever breaks (spares face); classic cause of febrile seizures in infants.

name: Human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8 / KSHV)
morphology: Spindle cells on biopsy; LANA-1 immunostain. Associated with HIV/AIDS, MSM, and Mediterranean/Sub-Saharan endemic forms.
diseases: Kaposi sarcoma; primary effusion (body cavity) lymphoma; HHV-8-associated multicentric Castleman disease.
treatment: Antiretroviral therapy (restore immunity); chemo (e.g., liposomal doxorubicin)/radiation for extensive KS.
pearl: Kaposi = violaceous vascular skin/mucosal lesions in AIDS. Vascular tumor (vs bacillary angiomatosis = Bartonella, neutrophilic infiltrate).

name: Human papillomavirus (HPV)
morphology: Koilocytes (perinuclear halo, raisinoid/wrinkled nuclei) on Pap smear/biopsy. E6 (degrades p53), E7 (inhibits Rb).
diseases: Common/plantar warts (1,2,4); condyloma acuminata (6,11); cervical/anal/vulvar/oropharyngeal/penile cancer (16,18).
treatment: Cryotherapy, imiquimod, podophyllin/salicylic acid for warts; Gardasil-9 vaccine (prevention); LEEP/cone for high-grade dysplasia.
pearl: 16 & 18 = high-risk oncogenic; 6 & 11 = anogenital warts/laryngeal papillomatosis. E6/E7 inactivate p53/Rb.

name: Hepatitis B virus (HBV)
type: Enveloped, partially dsDNA (circular) virus (Hepadnaviridae); uses reverse transcriptase
morphology: 'Ground-glass' hepatocytes; serology (HBsAg, anti-HBs, HBeAg, anti-HBe, anti-HBc). Dane particle = complete infectious virion.
diseases: Acute & chronic hepatitis; cirrhosis; hepatocellular carcinoma; serum-sickness-like syndrome, PAN, membranous GN.
treatment: Entecavir, tenofovir, peginterferon-alpha; recombinant HBsAg vaccine (prevention).
pearl: Only DNA virus using reverse transcriptase. Anti-HBc IgM = sole marker in window period. HBeAg = high infectivity/replication.

name: Adenovirus
morphology: Naked icosahedral capsid with penton-base fibers; basophilic 'smudge cell' intranuclear inclusions. Cytopathic effect in culture.
diseases: Febrile pharyngoconjunctival fever; epidemic keratoconjunctivitis ('pink eye'); URI/pneumonia; hemorrhagic cystitis; gastroenteritis (serotypes 40/41).
treatment: Supportive (self-limited); cidofovir for severe disease in immunocompromised; live oral vaccine for military recruits.
pearl: Outbreaks in military barracks, swimming pools ('pool conjunctivitis'). Non-enveloped = survives chlorination, fomites.

name: Parvovirus B19
type: Non-enveloped, linear ssDNA virus (Parvoviridae); smallest DNA virus
morphology: Binds P antigen (globoside) on RBC precursors; PCR/serology (IgM). Infects erythroid progenitors in marrow.
diseases: Erythema infectiosum (fifth disease); transient aplastic crisis in chronic hemolysis (e.g., sickle cell); hydrops fetalis; arthropathy in adults.
treatment: Supportive; IVIG for chronic infection/PRCA in immunocompromised; intrauterine transfusion for hydrops fetalis.
pearl: 'Slapped-cheek' rash in kids (lacy reticular rash on body). Aplastic crisis in chronic hemolytic anemia; pure red cell aplasia (drops Hb).

name: Poxvirus (Smallpox / Molluscum contagiosum)
type: Enveloped, linear dsDNA virus (Poxviridae); largest/most complex DNA virus, brick-shaped
morphology: Replicates in CYTOPLASM (carries own DNA-dependent RNA polymerase). Guarnieri bodies (variola); Molluscum: Henderson-Paterson cytoplasmic inclusion bodies.
diseases: Smallpox (variola, eradicated 1980); molluscum contagiosum (umbilicated papules); mpox (monkeypox); cowpox; orf.
treatment: Tecovirimat (smallpox/mpox); molluscum self-limited or curettage/cryotherapy/cantharidin.
pearl: Only DNA virus replicating in cytoplasm. Molluscum = flesh-colored umbilicated papules; widespread/giant lesions in HIV/AIDS.

name: Influenza virus (A/B)
type: Virus — enveloped, (-) ssRNA, segmented (8 segments), Orthomyxovirus
morphology: Helical nucleocapsid; surface HA (attachment/hemagglutination) & NA (release) spikes; 8-segment genome enables reassortment
diseases: Influenza: fever, myalgias, dry cough; complications—primary viral pneumonia, secondary bacterial pneumonia (S. aureus, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae)
treatment: Oseltamivir/zanamivir/peramivir (NA inhibitors), baloxavir (endonuclease inhibitor); annual vaccine (killed IM or live attenuated intranasal)
pearl: Antigenic DRIFT (minor point mutations→epidemics) vs SHIFT (major reassortment→pandemics). Only segmented genomes reassort; Reye syndrome if given aspirin.

name: Rhinovirus
morphology: >100 serotypes; acid-LABILE (destroyed by stomach acid, unlike enterovirus); replicates best at 33°C (cooler nasal passages); binds ICAM-1
diseases: Common cold (most frequent cause); can trigger asthma/COPD exacerbations
treatment: Supportive only
pearl: Acid-labile + cooler-temp preference = why it stays in the nose. Binds ICAM-1. #1 cause of the common cold.

name: Poliovirus
morphology: Acid-STABLE (survives stomach, fecal-oral); replicates in GI/oropharynx then spreads to CNS anterior horn cells
diseases: Mostly asymptomatic/minor febrile illness; aseptic meningitis; flaccid paralysis (LMN, asymmetric) destroying anterior horn motor neurons; bulbar→respiratory failure
treatment: Supportive; prevent with Salk (IPV, killed/IM) or Sabin (OPV, live oral—gives mucosal IgA but rare vaccine-associated paralytic polio)
pearl: Asymmetric flaccid paralysis + intact sensation. Sabin OPV→herd immunity but can revert to virulence (VAPP/cVDPV).

name: Coxsackievirus (A & B)
morphology: Acid-stable, fecal-oral; type A vs B distinguish clinical syndromes
diseases: A: hand-foot-mouth disease, herpangina, acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis. B: myocarditis, pericarditis, dilated cardiomyopathy, pleurodynia (Bornholm/'devil's grip'); both→aseptic meningitis; A24→associated with T1DM
treatment: Supportive
pearl: Coxsackie B → 'B' for Body/heart (myocarditis/pleurodynia). Hand-foot-mouth = vesicles on palms, soles, oral mucosa. Major cause of viral (aseptic) meningitis.

name: Hepatitis A virus (HAV)
morphology: Acid-stable, fecal-oral; no chronic carrier state; anti-HAV IgM = acute infection, IgG = recovery/immunity
diseases: Acute self-limited hepatitis; often from contaminated water/shellfish/travel/daycare; no chronicity, no cirrhosis, no carcinoma; rare fulminant hepatitis
treatment: Supportive; killed (inactivated) vaccine; post-exposure prophylaxis with vaccine ± Ig
pearl: 'A = Asymptomatic (esp. kids)/Acute/Alimentary.' Short incubation, no chronic state. Travelers, shellfish, daycare.

name: Measles virus (Rubeola)
morphology: Single serotype; H & F surface glycoproteins (no neuraminidase); F protein→multinucleated Warthin-Finkeldey giant cells
diseases: Measles: high fever, 3 C's (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis), Koplik spots, then cephalocaudal maculopapular rash; complications—pneumonia (#1 cause of death), encephalitis, SSPE (years later); immune amnesia
treatment: Supportive + vitamin A; prevent with MMR (live attenuated)
pearl: Koplik spots (blue-white buccal spots) precede rash = pathognomonic. SSPE = late fatal sequela. Vitamin A reduces mortality.

name: Mumps virus
morphology: Single serotype; HN (hemagglutinin-neuraminidase) & F (fusion) glycoproteins
diseases: Parotitis (often bilateral), orchitis (sterility risk in post-pubertal males), aseptic meningitis/meningoencephalitis, pancreatitis; deafness
treatment: Supportive; prevent with MMR (live attenuated)
pearl: 'Mumps makes your parotid glands and testes as big as POM-poms.' Parotitis + orchitis + aseptic meningitis. Has HN (unlike measles).

name: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV)
type: Virus — enveloped, (-) ssRNA, helical, Pneumovirus (Pneumoviridae; formerly Paramyxovirus family)
morphology: F (fusion) protein→fused multinucleated syncytia; NO hemagglutinin/neuraminidase (G protein for attachment, unlike other paramyxos)
diseases: #1 cause of bronchiolitis & pneumonia in infants <1 yr; wheezing, respiratory distress, apnea; URI in older children/adults
treatment: Supportive (ribavirin reserved/rarely used); prevent with palivizumab or nirsevimab (anti-F mAbs) in high-risk infants; maternal/older-adult vaccines available
pearl: Syncytia formation. Bronchiolitis in infants <1 yr. Nirsevimab/palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk/preemies.

name: Rabies virus
type: Virus — enveloped, (-) ssRNA, helical, bullet-shaped, Rhabdovirus (Lyssavirus)
morphology: Bullet-shaped virion; Negri bodies = eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions in hippocampal (pyramidal) & cerebellar Purkinje neurons
diseases: Rabies: long/variable incubation, prodrome, then encephalitic (furious: hydrophobia, pharyngeal spasms, agitation) or paralytic form→coma/death; binds nAChR, travels retrograde up peripheral nerves to CNS
treatment: Post-exposure prophylaxis: wound cleaning + rabies Ig (into wound) + killed vaccine series (effective before symptoms); near-100% fatal once symptomatic
pearl: Negri bodies (cytoplasmic, pathognomonic). Bat (most common US source)/dog/raccoon. Retrograde axonal transport. Hydrophobia.

name: Flavivirus group (Dengue, Zika, Yellow fever, WNV)
type: Virus — enveloped, (+) ssRNA, icosahedral, Flavivirus (mostly arboviruses)
morphology: Arthropod-borne (Aedes aegypti for dengue/Zika/YF; Culex for WNV/SLE); Zika also sexual/vertical transmission; detect via serology/RT-PCR
diseases: Dengue: breakbone fever, retro-orbital pain; severe dengue/DHF-DSS on 2nd heterotypic infection (ADE). Zika: congenital microcephaly, Guillain-Barré. Yellow fever: jaundice, black vomit, Councilman bodies. WNV: encephalitis (esp. elderly), flaccid paralysis
treatment: Supportive (avoid NSAIDs/aspirin in dengue); Yellow fever live-attenuated vaccine; dengue vaccine (Dengvaxia) only for seropositive
pearl: Dengue antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE)→worse 2nd infection. Zika→microcephaly. Yellow fever→Councilman (apoptotic) bodies.

name: Hepatitis C virus (HCV)
type: Virus — enveloped, (+) ssRNA, icosahedral, Flavivirus family (Hepacivirus)
morphology: High genomic variability (no proofreading RNA polymerase, hypervariable E2 envelope)→immune evasion, no effective vaccine; dx by anti-HCV Ab screen then HCV RNA PCR (confirm/quantify)
diseases: Chronic hepatitis (~80% of acute→chronic), cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma; extrahepatic—mixed cryoglobulinemia, membranoproliferative GN, porphyria cutanea tarda
treatment: Direct-acting antivirals (e.g., sofosbuvir + ledipasvir or velpatasvir)—curative >95%; no vaccine
pearl: 'C = Chronic.' Lack of proofreading→antigenic variation→no vaccine. DAAs are curative. Cryoglobulinemia. Bloodborne (IVDU).

name: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus)
type: Virus — enveloped, (+) ssRNA, diploid (2 copies), Retrovirus (Lentivirus)
morphology: Reverse transcriptase, integrase, protease; gp120 (attachment to CD4 + co-receptor CCR5/CXCR4) & gp41 (fusion); p24 capsid; screen 4th-gen Ag/Ab combo, confirm with Ab differentiation assay (+/- RNA), Western blot now largely replaced
diseases: AIDS (CD4 <200 or AIDS-defining illness): opportunistic infections (PCP <200, toxo/cryptococcus <100, CMV/MAC <50), Kaposi sarcoma (HHV-8), CNS lymphoma (EBV), HIV dementia
treatment: ART: 2 NRTIs + integrase strand-transfer inhibitor (e.g., tenofovir/emtricitabine + dolutegravir or bictegravir); PrEP for prevention
pearl: Diploid (+) ssRNA but replicates via DNA intermediate. CCR5-Δ32 homozygotes resistant. p24 antigen earliest serum marker (after RNA).

name: Rotavirus
type: Virus — naked, dsRNA, segmented (11 segments), double-shelled wheel-shaped, Reovirus
morphology: Wheel-like (rota=wheel) on EM; double protein capsid; 11 dsRNA segments; carries own RNA-dependent RNA polymerase; fecal-oral, environmentally stable
diseases: #1 cause of severe infantile/childhood gastroenteritis worldwide; watery (osmotic + secretory) diarrhea→dehydration; NSP4 acts as viral enterotoxin
treatment: Oral rehydration; prevent with live oral attenuated vaccine (slight intussusception risk)
pearl: Wheel-shaped virion. Leading cause of fatal childhood diarrhea (esp. winter). NSP4 = first described viral enterotoxin.

name: Coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2 / MERS / SARS)
type: Virus — enveloped, (+) ssRNA (largest RNA genome), helical nucleocapsid, Coronavirus
morphology: Club-shaped spike (S) glycoproteins → 'corona/crown' on EM; SARS-CoV-2 S protein binds ACE2 (MERS binds DPP4); dx by RT-PCR/antigen
diseases: COVID-19: fever, dry cough, anosmia/ageusia, ARDS, hypercoagulable state, MIS-C in children; endemic coronaviruses cause common cold/URI
treatment: Supportive; nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid), remdesivir, dexamethasone in severe/hypoxic; mRNA & other vaccines
pearl: Spike binds ACE2 (SARS-CoV-2). Largest RNA genome. 'Crown' of spikes on EM. Anosmia is a tip-off; has proofreading exonuclease (rare among RNA viruses).

name: Candida albicans
type: Fungus (yeast; thermally dimorphic-like, forms pseudohyphae/true hyphae)
morphology: Germ tube positive at 37C; budding yeast + pseudohyphae at 20C, true hyphae/germ tubes at 37C; chlamydospores on cornmeal/Tween agar
diseases: Oral thrush, esophagitis, vulvovaginitis, diaper rash, intertrigo, candidemia/disseminated disease, endocarditis (IVDU), chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis
treatment: Nystatin or fluconazole (mucocutaneous); echinocandin (caspofungin/micafungin) for invasive/candidemia; amphotericin B for severe
pearl: Germ tube positive. Oral thrush in HIV at CD4<200; esophagitis is AIDS-defining. Disseminated in neutropenics; IVDU/indwelling-line endocarditis.

name: Aspergillus fumigatus
morphology: Septate hyphae branching at acute (~45-degree) V-shaped angles; conidiophores with radiating chains of conidia; NOT dimorphic
diseases: Invasive aspergillosis (angioinvasive lung/sinus), aspergilloma (fungus ball) in old cavity, ABPA, allergic sinusitis; aflatoxin (A. flavus) linked to hepatocellular carcinoma
treatment: Voriconazole (first-line invasive); isavuconazole or liposomal amphotericin B alternatives; surgery/embolization for aspergilloma; steroids +/- itraconazole for ABPA
pearl: Acute ~45-degree branching septate hyphae. Aspergilloma = fungus ball in old TB cavity; air-crescent / Monod sign, mobile with positioning. ABPA in asthma/CF with high IgE & eosinophilia.

name: Cryptococcus neoformans
morphology: Heavily encapsulated narrow-based budding yeast; India ink shows clear halo; mucicarmine stains capsule red; melanin/Fontana-Masson positive; urease positive; CrAg latex agglutination antigen test
diseases: Cryptococcal meningitis/meningoencephalitis (esp HIV CD4<100), pneumonia; C. gattii affects immunocompetent (Pacific NW/eucalyptus)
treatment: Amphotericin B + flucytosine (induction), then fluconazole consolidation/maintenance; serial LPs for elevated ICP
pearl: India ink halo; 'soap bubble' brain lesions. Urease+ and melanin+ (laccase). Pigeon/bird droppings & soil. Serum/CSF CrAg is most sensitive & specific test.

name: Histoplasma capsulatum
morphology: Mold in soil/cold (tuberculate macroconidia + microconidia), yeast in tissue at 37C; small oval yeast clustered INSIDE macrophages
diseases: Pulmonary histoplasmosis (TB-like, can calcify), disseminated disease in immunocompromised with hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, oral/tongue ulcers, adrenal involvement
treatment: Itraconazole (mild-moderate); liposomal amphotericin B (severe/disseminated/CNS)
pearl: Macrophages stuffed with intracellular yeast. Mississippi/Ohio River valleys; bird & BAT droppings, caves/spelunking. Urine/serum antigen for diagnosis.

name: Blastomyces dermatitidis
morphology: Mold in cold, yeast in tissue at 37C; large broad-based budding yeast with thick, doubly-refractile wall, roughly same size as the nucleus
diseases: Pulmonary blastomycosis (acute/chronic pneumonia), verrucous/ulcerated skin lesions, osteomyelitis, GU disease (prostatitis)
treatment: Itraconazole (mild-moderate); liposomal amphotericin B (severe/CNS/disseminated)
pearl: Broad-based budding ('Big Bad Blasto, broad base'). Great Lakes, Ohio & Mississippi River basins, eastern US. Granulomatous skin lesions mimic SCC.

name: Coccidioides immitis / posadasii
morphology: Mold in soil (barrel-shaped arthroconidia/arthrospores), large spherules packed with endospores in tissue
diseases: Valley fever (pneumonia), erythema nodosum + arthralgias ('desert rheumatism / bumps') as hypersensitivity, disseminated disease incl meningitis (risk: dark-skinned/Filipino, pregnancy 3rd trimester, immunocompromised)
treatment: Often self-limited; fluconazole or itraconazole; amphotericin B for severe; LIFELONG fluconazole for meningitis (intrathecal ampho B if refractory)
pearl: Spherules with endospores. Southwestern US (Arizona, California San Joaquin Valley); arthroconidia inhaled after dust storms, earthquakes, archaeology digs.

name: Paracoccidioides brasiliensis
morphology: Mold in cold, yeast in tissue at 37C; large central yeast with multiple narrow-based buds = 'captain's wheel' / 'mariner's wheel' (pilot's wheel) morphology
diseases: Paracoccidioidomycosis: chronic pulmonary disease, mucocutaneous (oral/nasal) ulcers, lymphadenopathy
treatment: Itraconazole; amphotericin B for severe disease (sulfonamides historically)
pearl: 'Captain's/pilot's wheel' budding yeast. Rural Latin America (esp Brazil); affects adult men (estrogen is protective, so symptomatic disease rare in women).

name: Sporothrix schenckii
morphology: Mold with rosette/'daisy'-arranged conidia on delicate conidiophores in cold; cigar-shaped budding yeast in tissue at 37C
diseases: Sporotrichosis: subcutaneous nodule/papule that ulcerates, then ascending nodular lesions along lymphatics (nodular lymphangitis); rarely pulmonary/disseminated
treatment: Itraconazole (first-line); saturated potassium iodide (SSKI) solution (classic/low-cost); amphotericin B if disseminated/severe
pearl: 'Rose gardener's disease' — thorn prick / sphagnum moss / gardening. Cigar-shaped yeast; nodules ascend along lymphatic chain.

name: Pneumocystis jirovecii
type: Fungus (atypical; historically classified/treated as a protozoan)
morphology: Cannot be cultured; cup/disc-shaped cysts ('crushed ping-pong balls') on methenamine silver (GMS), toluidine blue, or DFA; obtained via BAL or induced sputum
diseases: Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP): diffuse bilateral interstitial 'ground-glass' pneumonia in AIDS (CD4<200) and other immunocompromised
treatment: TMP-SMX (treatment & prophylaxis); ADD corticosteroids if PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >=35; alternatives pentamidine, atovaquone, clindamycin-primaquine, dapsone
pearl: AIDS-defining; start prophylaxis at CD4<200. Diffuse bilateral ground-glass infiltrates, elevated LDH, beta-D-glucan positive; disc/cup-shaped cysts on silver stain.

name: Mucor / Rhizopus (Mucormycosis)
morphology: Broad, ribbon-like NON-septate (sparsely septate/aseptate) hyphae branching at wide (~90-degree) right angles
diseases: Rhinocerebral mucormycosis (DKA/diabetics), pulmonary (neutropenic), cutaneous, GI; angioinvasive with tissue necrosis
treatment: Liposomal amphotericin B (or isavuconazole/posaconazole) + aggressive surgical debridement; reverse underlying DKA/acidosis/neutropenia
pearl: Wide-angle (~90-degree) aseptate ribbon-like hyphae. DKA & neutropenia; iron overload / deferoxamine therapy is a risk factor. Black necrotic eschar on palate/turbinates.

name: Malassezia furfur
morphology: 'Spaghetti and meatballs' / 'ziti and meatballs' on KOH: short curved hyphae + clusters of round yeast; KOH of skin scrapings
diseases: Tinea (pityriasis) versicolor (hypo/hyperpigmented patches), seborrheic dermatitis/dandruff, catheter-related fungemia (esp neonates/adults on lipid-rich TPN)
treatment: Topical selenium sulfide, topical/oral azoles (ketoconazole), or terbinafine
pearl: 'Spaghetti and meatballs.' Lipophilic; produces azelaic acid that inhibits tyrosinase causing hypopigmented patches that fail to tan. Worse in hot/humid weather.

name: Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton)
morphology: Septate hyphae on KOH of skin/hair/nail; culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar; some Microsporum fluoresce green under Wood lamp
diseases: Tinea (corporis/ringworm, pedis/athlete's foot, cruris/jock itch, capitis, unguium/onychomycosis); pruritic ring with central clearing
treatment: Topical azoles or terbinafine; oral terbinafine or griseofulvin for nail (unguium) & scalp (capitis)
pearl: Pruritic ring with central clearing; KOH prep of scrapings. Trichophyton is most common cause; only some Microsporum (not Trichophyton) fluoresce on Wood lamp.

name: Candida (non-albicans, esp. C. glabrata / C. krusei / C. auris)
morphology: Budding yeast; germ tube NEGATIVE; C. glabrata small with no pseudohyphae; identify by chromogenic agar / MALDI-TOF; C. auris is multidrug-resistant & hard to ID
diseases: Candidemia, UTI, mucosal candidiasis (often azole-resistant); C. auris causes nosocomial outbreaks
treatment: Echinocandin preferred (esp C. glabrata/krusei/auris); C. krusei is intrinsically fluconazole-resistant; C. auris often multidrug-resistant
pearl: Think azole resistance: C. krusei = intrinsic fluconazole resistance; rises after prior azole exposure. Echinocandins are go-to; C. auris is an emerging MDR nosocomial threat.

name: Plasmodium falciparum
morphology: Thick/thin Giemsa blood smear; banana/crescent-shaped gametocytes; multiply-infected RBCs; normal-sized RBCs; high parasitemia; rings only, no later forms; no Schuffner dots; Maurer's clefts
diseases: Severe (malignant) malaria: cerebral malaria, blackwater fever, irregular/continuous fevers; most deadly Plasmodium species
treatment: Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT); IV artesunate for severe disease
pearl: Cytoadherence/rosetting -> cerebral malaria; sickle trait & HbC protective. No hypnozoites = no true relapse (recrudescence only).

name: Plasmodium vivax / ovale
